name: B.S. in Computer Science and B.S. in Mathematical Sciences, Applied Mathematics

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: Within the visible terms the sequence is sound: the CS spine builds logically (CS 114 to CS 280 to CS 288 to CS 350 to CS 435/CS 341) and the math spine progresses through Calculus III, Differential Equations, and Vector Analysis before capstone work (CS 491 Senior Project). As a 5-year combined degree the heavier load and longer horizon are expected. Two issues hold it to adequate: the parsed plan begins at 'Second Year - 1st Semester', so the entire first-year foundation (intro CS, Calc I/II, intro physics) is absent from the shown progression; and the plan is conspicuously physics-heavy (PHYS 234/335/430/432/442/485, OPSE 310) for a program titled CS + Applied Mathematics, with relatively few upper-level applied-math courses actually appearing in the plan of study, so the mapping between the two named degrees is not obvious.
dimensions.suggestion: Add the missing first-year term and clearly label which courses satisfy the CS degree versus the Applied Mathematics degree so the dual-degree progression is legible end to end.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The deepest prerequisite chain is 7, deep but reasonable for a 5-year combined degree, and the sample prerequisite strings are well-formed with explicit boolean logic and grade thresholds. However, missingCourseCount is 8 with dangling references CHEM 126A, GER 200, GER 300, MATH 300, MATH 400, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A, and PHYS 231A. Most are placeholder/category codes (GER 200/300, MATH 300/400 elective tiers) or lab co-sections (CHEM 126A, PHYS 111A/121A/231A) rather than genuinely broken course prerequisites, which softens the impact but still leaves the graph incompletely resolved.
dimensions.suggestion: Map the 8 dangling codes to real catalog entries or explicitly mark GER/elective tiers as category placeholders so the prerequisite graph resolves cleanly.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: The provided data contains no program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives - only a plan of study and course list. For a catalog page, the absence of stated outcomes is a real finding: prospective students and reviewers cannot see what competencies the combined CS + Applied Mathematics degree is designed to produce.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish an explicit set of program-level learning outcomes/educational objectives for the combined degree, ideally distinguishing the computing and applied-mathematics competencies.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The stated total of 134 credits is appropriate for a 5-year combined dual degree. However, creditReconcileDelta is -33 (summed term credits 101 versus stated 134), a large discrepancy driven primarily by the parsed plan omitting the first-year term rather than by a curriculum flaw. As presented, the plan does not reconcile to its stated total, which undermines transparency of how credits are distributed across general education, CS core, math, and physics.
dimensions.suggestion: Include the missing first-year term so summed term credits reconcile to the stated 134, and show a summary breakdown by category (GER, CS core, math/applied-math, physics, electives).

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The plan is organized into labeled terms with per-term credit totals, which helps. But the page has notable parsing/formatting defects: catalog URLs are split across multiple rows as fragmentary null-code items (e.g., 'History and Humanities GER 200 level (http://catalog...' followed by 'general-education-requirements/ger-200-level/)'), and 'or ...' alternatives are rendered as standalone null-code line items rather than being attached to their base course. There is also no program-level overview/description and no first-year term, leaving the page incomplete and harder to read.
dimensions.suggestion: Repair the parsed rows so alternative courses ('or MATH 328', 'or COM 313') and split URLs render as part of their parent requirement, and add an introductory program description.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: Required and elective coursework reflects current practice well: CS 301 Introduction to Data Science, CS 351 Introduction to Cybersecurity, CS 356 Computer Networks, CS 435 Advanced Data Structures and Algorithms, CS 331 Databases, and CS 490/491 software-engineering and senior-project work, complemented by strong computational math/physics (MATH 340 Applied Numerical Methods, PHYS 485 Computer Modeling of Applied Physics Problems). The main modern gap is the absence of a dedicated machine-learning/AI course and any cloud/DevOps exposure, but the data-science plus cybersecurity plus computational-modeling combination is contemporary and marketable.
dimensions.suggestion: Add or cross-list a machine-learning/AI elective to close the most visible modern gap and reinforce the data-science emphasis.

descriptionQuality.justification: Sample course descriptions are consistently present, specific, and informative rather than thin or placeholder - e.g., CS 241, CS 301, CS 341, CS 435, and the MATH sequence all carry substantive topic-level detail. A minor blemish is bleed-through text in a few excerpts (MATH 222's excerpt runs into unrelated 'MATH 225. Survey of Probability...' content), but overall description quality is strong.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Stated prerequisites are generally clear and internally consistent, using explicit grade conditions ('with a grade C or better') and sensible AND/OR logic (e.g., CS 341: '(CS 241 or MATH 226) and CS 280'). Clarity is reduced by the 8 dangling references at the program level (labs and GER/elective placeholders such as PHYS 231A, GER 200/300, MATH 300/400) and by 'or' alternatives that appear as detached null-code items, which weaken end-to-end consistency.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program-level learning outcomes/educational objectives for the combined CS + Applied Mathematics degree, distinguishing the computing and applied-math competencies.
Reconcile the credit plan: add the missing first-year term so summed credits meet the stated 134 (currently 101, creditReconcileDelta -33) and present a category-level credit breakdown.

Resolve the 8 dangling course references (CHEM 126A, GER 200, GER 300, MATH 300, MATH 400, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A, PHYS 231A) by mapping labs/electives to real codes or flagging them as category placeholders.
Fix presentation defects: reattach 'or ...' alternatives and split catalog URLs to their parent requirements and add an introductory program description.
Clarify the dual-degree structure by labeling which courses satisfy CS versus Applied Mathematics, since the visible plan reads as physics-heavy relative to the program title.
